name: Electric Charges and Fields
subject: Physics

sections.heading: 1. Electric Charge and its Properties
sections.body:
Electric charge is an intrinsic property of matter responsible for electromagnetic interactions. There are two kinds: positive (e.g. charge on glass rubbed with silk) and negative (e.g. on plastic rubbed with fur). Like charges repel; unlike charges attract.

Three fundamental properties:

  • Additivity: total charge of a system is the algebraic sum of individual charges.
  • Conservation: net charge of an isolated system is constant; charge can be transferred but never created or destroyed.
  • Quantisation: charge exists in integer multiples of the elementary charge $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, so $q = ne$ for some integer $n$. Quarks carry $\pm e/3$ or $\pm 2e/3$ but are never isolated.

SI unit of charge is the coulomb (C). A body becomes charged by transfer of electrons; gaining electrons gives a negative charge, losing them gives a positive charge.

sections.heading: 2. Coulomb's Law
sections.body:
The electrostatic force between two stationary point charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ separated by distance $r$ in vacuum is $$F = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$$ directed along the line joining them. The proportionality constant $k = 1/(4\pi\varepsilon_0) \approx 9 \times 10^9\ \text{N m}^2/\text{C}^2$, with $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C}^2/\text{N m}^2$ the permittivity of free space.

In a medium of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ (dielectric constant $K$), the force becomes $F = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$, weaker than in vacuum.

In vector form, the force on $q_2$ due to $q_1$ is $\vec{F}_{21} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}\hat{r}_{21}$, where $\hat{r}_{21}$ points from 1 to 2. Coulomb's law obeys Newton's third law: $\vec{F}_{12} = -\vec{F}_{21}$.

sections.heading: 3. Superposition Principle
sections.body:
The total force on a charge due to several other charges is the vector sum of forces exerted by each, computed pairwise as if the others were absent. For charge $q_1$ in the presence of $q_2, q_3, \ldots q_n$: $$\vec{F}_1 = \sum_{i=2}^{n} \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{q_1 q_i}{r_{1i}^2}\hat{r}_{1i}$$ The principle holds because Coulomb's law is linear in charge.

For a continuous charge distribution, the sum becomes an integral over linear, surface, or volume charge densities $\lambda$, $\sigma$, $\rho$ respectively.

sections.heading: 4. Electric Field
sections.body:
The electric field at a point is the force per unit positive test charge placed there: $$\vec{E} = \lim_{q_0 \to 0} \dfrac{\vec{F}}{q_0}$$ The test charge must be small so it does not disturb the source distribution. Unit: N/C or V/m.

Field due to a point charge $q$ at distance $r$: $\vec{E} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{q}{r^2}\hat{r}$, pointing away from positive $q$ and toward negative $q$.

Field is a vector; superposition applies: $\vec{E}_{\text{total}} = \sum_i \vec{E}_i$. The field is independent of the test charge — it is a property of the source.

sections.heading: 5. Electric Field Lines
sections.body:
Electric field lines are imaginary curves whose tangent at every point gives the direction of $\vec{E}$ there, and whose density represents field strength.

Properties:

  • Begin on positive charges, end on negative charges (or extend to infinity).
  • Never form closed loops (electrostatic field is conservative).
  • Two field lines can never intersect — at intersection $\vec{E}$ would have two directions.
  • For a uniform field, lines are parallel and equally spaced.
  • Density of lines is proportional to $|\vec{E}|$.

sections.heading: 6. Electric Dipole
sections.body:
A pair of equal and opposite point charges $\pm q$ separated by a small distance $2a$ forms an electric dipole. The dipole moment is $\vec{p} = q \cdot 2a\,\hat{n}$, directed from $-q$ to $+q$. Unit: C·m.

Field on the axial line at distance $r$ from centre ($r \gg a$): $$E_{\text{axial}} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{2p}{r^3}$$ along $\vec{p}$.

Field on the equatorial line at distance $r$: $$E_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{p}{r^3}$$ anti-parallel to $\vec{p}$.

Note that dipole field falls off as $1/r^3$, faster than a point charge ($1/r^2$).

sections.heading: 7. Dipole in a Uniform External Field
sections.body:
A dipole in a uniform field $\vec{E}$ experiences zero net force (forces on $\pm q$ cancel) but a torque: $$\vec{\tau} = \vec{p} \times \vec{E}, \quad |\tau| = pE\sin\theta$$ where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{p}$ and $\vec{E}$. The torque tends to align $\vec{p}$ with $\vec{E}$.

Potential energy of orientation: $U = -\vec{p}\cdot\vec{E} = -pE\cos\theta$. Minimum energy when $\vec{p}\parallel\vec{E}$ (stable), maximum when antiparallel (unstable).

sections.heading: 8. Electric Flux and Gauss's Law
sections.body:
Electric flux through a surface: $\Phi_E = \int \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}$. For uniform field through flat area $A$: $\Phi = EA\cos\theta$. Unit: N m²/C or V·m.

Gauss's law: the total flux through any closed surface equals $1/\varepsilon_0$ times the net charge enclosed. $$\oint \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A} = \dfrac{q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}$$

Gauss's law is valid for any closed surface (Gaussian surface) and any charge distribution. It is most useful when the system has high symmetry (spherical, cylindrical, planar), where we can pull $E$ outside the integral.

sections.heading: 9. Applications of Gauss's Law
sections.body:
Infinite line charge with linear density $\lambda$: cylindrical Gaussian surface gives $$E = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$$ radially outward.

Infinite plane sheet with surface density $\sigma$: $$E = \dfrac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}$$ perpendicular to the sheet, independent of distance.

Uniformly charged spherical shell of radius $R$, total charge $Q$:

  • Outside ($r>R$): $E = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{Q}{r^2}$ — same as a point charge at the centre.
  • Inside ($r

For a conductor's surface: $E = \sigma/\varepsilon_0$ just outside, and $E = 0$ inside.

summary: Electric charge is quantised, conserved, and additive. Coulomb's law gives the inverse-square electrostatic force between point charges, modelled via the electric field $\vec{E} = \vec{F}/q_0$. A dipole experiences torque $\vec{p}\times\vec{E}$ in a uniform field with energy $-\vec{p}\cdot\vec{E}$. Gauss's law $\oint\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A} = q_{\text{enc}}/\varepsilon_0$ exploits symmetry to compute fields of lines, sheets, and shells.
pitfalls: Coulomb's law applies only to point charges (or spherically symmetric distributions). For other shapes, integrate.
Field lines never cross; if a problem asks why, the reason is single-valued direction of E.
Inside a uniformly charged spherical SHELL, E = 0; for a solid sphere, E grows linearly with r inside.
Gauss's law always holds, but is only USEFUL when symmetry lets you pull E out of the surface integral.
Dipole field falls as 1/r³, not 1/r²; many students forget the factor of 2 in axial vs equatorial.
Direction of dipole moment is from -q to +q (not +q to -q).
